human_request_text: Write an article about energy or climate topics such as new battery tech, grid infrastructure, renewable milestones, nuclear, or fusion

article.title: Largest US Offshore Wind Farm Delivers First Power After Surviving Trump Stop-Work Order and $230 Million Delay
article.summary: Dominion Energy's 2.6 GW Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project began sending electricity to the grid on March 23, months after a federal judge overturned a Trump administration stop-work order that cost the $11.5 billion project nearly $230 million.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The largest offshore wind farm in the United States has begun generating electricity. Dominion Energy's Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind project, a 2.6-gigawatt installation stretching 27 to 44 miles off the coast of Virginia Beach, delivered its first power to the regional grid on March 23, 2026, according to [Offshore Wind](https://www.offshorewind.biz/2026/03/24/first-power-flows-from-biggest-us-offshore-wind-farm/). The milestone came after a turbulent period that included a federal stop-work order, a lawsuit against the Trump administration, and a cost increase of roughly $300 million.

## What We Know

The project's first fully completed turbine, a 14-megawatt Siemens Gamesa unit, began spinning and feeding power to the grid in late March. When all 176 turbines are installed and operational, the farm is expected to produce enough electricity to power approximately 660,000 homes, [Electrek reported](https://electrek.co/2026/03/25/us-largest-wind-farm-just-produced-power-for-the-first-time/).

Construction is now more than 70 percent complete. All 176 monopile foundations have been installed, and multiple export cables connecting the offshore array to the onshore grid are in place, according to [gCaptain](https://gcaptain.com/dominions-11-5b-virginia-offshore-wind-project-delivers-first-power-after-legal-fight-cost-surge/). The turbines are being installed by the *Charybdis*, described as the first Jones Act-compliant wind turbine installation vessel in the United States, built at Seatrium's AmFELS shipyard in Brownsville, Texas, according to [Offshore Wind](https://www.offshorewind.biz/2026/03/24/first-power-flows-from-biggest-us-offshore-wind-farm/). Full completion is expected in early 2027.

The total project cost has risen to $11.5 billion, up from an original estimate of $9.8 billion. According to [gCaptain](https://gcaptain.com/dominions-11-5b-virginia-offshore-wind-project-delivers-first-power-after-legal-fight-cost-surge/), $228 million of the increase is attributable to a 25-day stop-work order imposed by the Trump administration in December 2025, during which the project incurred roughly $5 million per day in costs from equipment storage, contractual penalties, an idle workforce, and vessel delays. An additional $137 million in costs stems from tariffs, with total tariff exposure projected at $580 million through early 2027.

## The Legal Battle

On December 22, 2025, the U.S. Department of the Interior issued a stop-work order against CVOW and four other offshore wind projects along the East Coast, citing unspecified national security concerns related to potential radar interference, according to [gCaptain](https://gcaptain.com/dominions-11-5b-virginia-offshore-wind-project-delivers-first-power-after-legal-fight-cost-surge/). Dominion Energy sued the federal government, and on January 16, 2026, a federal judge in Norfolk granted a preliminary injunction allowing construction to resume. The court found the suspension "overly broad," according to [gCaptain](https://gcaptain.com/dominions-11-5b-virginia-offshore-wind-project-delivers-first-power-after-legal-fight-cost-surge/).

Dominion CEO Robert Blue said the project delivered its first power "right on schedule" and described it as "a critical part of Virginia's all-of-the-above energy strategy," addressing what he called "the fastest-growing power demand in the country," as reported by [Electrek](https://electrek.co/2026/03/25/us-largest-wind-farm-just-produced-power-for-the-first-time/).

## Broader Industry Context

The CVOW milestone arrives at a moment of tension for the U.S. offshore wind industry. While the Trump administration has moved to obstruct offshore wind development, multiple projects have secured legal victories enabling them to continue. According to [CleanTechnica](https://cleantechnica.com/2026/03/25/sierra-club-celebrates-as-the-coastal-virginia-offshore-wind-project-powers-on/), two additional East Coast projects have advanced since the January injunction: Vineyard Wind off the Massachusetts coast has completed construction, and Revolution Wind off Connecticut and Rhode Island has begun delivering power to the grid.

Environmental groups have framed the milestone as a rebuke to federal opposition. Nancy Pyne, Senior Advisor at the Sierra Club, said CVOW's progress "proves that in the face of obstacles and challenges put up by Donald Trump, offshore wind will prevail," according to [CleanTechnica](https://cleantechnica.com/2026/03/25/sierra-club-celebrates-as-the-coastal-virginia-offshore-wind-project-powers-on/). Connor Kish, Director of the Sierra Club Virginia Chapter, called it "a remarkable step toward cleaner, more affordable energy in Virginia," citing the project's insulation against energy price volatility, as reported by [CleanTechnica](https://cleantechnica.com/2026/03/25/sierra-club-celebrates-as-the-coastal-virginia-offshore-wind-project-powers-on/).

Dominion projects that the wind farm will save customers $3 billion during its first decade of operation, according to [CleanTechnica](https://cleantechnica.com/2026/03/25/sierra-club-celebrates-as-the-coastal-virginia-offshore-wind-project-powers-on/).

## What We Don't Know

Several uncertainties remain. The final cost of the project is not yet fixed, as tariff exposure is projected to continue accumulating through early 2027 and could reach $580 million, according to [gCaptain](https://gcaptain.com/dominions-11-5b-virginia-offshore-wind-project-delivers-first-power-after-legal-fight-cost-surge/). Whether the Trump administration will pursue further legal or regulatory actions against CVOW or other offshore wind projects is unclear. The status of the underlying lawsuit between Dominion and the federal government has not been resolved; only the preliminary injunction has been decided.

The broader question of how federal policy will affect the U.S. offshore wind pipeline also remains open. While CVOW, Vineyard Wind, and Revolution Wind have advanced, several other proposed projects along the Atlantic coast face uncertain regulatory futures.
